quiz_questions.question_text: What is an electric field?

options.text: A region where a charged particle experiences a force
The space inside a capacitor only
A magnetic field around a wire
A region with no charges

quiz_questions.explanation: An electric field is a region where a charged particle experiences a force. Field strength: E = kQ/r².

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for electric field?

options.text: E = F/q = kQ/r²
E = kQ/r
E = Fq
E = FR²/q

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric field E = F/q = kQ/r² for a point charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: Field lines point away from:

options.text: Both equally
Neither
Negative charges
Positive charges

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is electric potential?

options.text: Current per unit time
Energy per unit charge: V = U/q
Force per unit charge
Charge per unit energy

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric potential is the energy per unit charge: V = U/q = kQ/r.

quiz_questions.question_text: Equipotential lines are perpendicular to:

options.text: Magnetic field lines
Current-carrying wires
Electric field lines
Gravitational field lines

quiz_questions.explanation: Equipotential lines are always perpendicular to electric field lines. No work is done moving charge along an equipotential.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the units of electric field?

options.text: J/C or W/A
C/m or A·s
Pa or N/m²
N/C or V/m

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric field has units of Newtons per Coulomb (N/C) or equivalently Volts per meter (V/m).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the unit of electric potential?

options.text: Farad (F)
Ampere (A)
Ohm (Ω)
Volt (V) = J/C

quiz_questions.explanation: The unit of electric potential is the Volt (V), where 1 V = 1 J/C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the electric field inside a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium?

options.text: It equals the external field strength
It is at its maximum possible value
It is zero throughout the conductor
It depends on the shape of the conductor

quiz_questions.explanation: In electrostatic equilibrium, the electric field inside a conductor is zero. If there were a field, charges would move until the field is canceled.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which direction does a positive charge move in an electric field?

options.text: Perpendicular to the field line direction
From low potential to high potential regions
From high potential to low potential regions
In a circular path around the field lines

quiz_questions.explanation: A positive charge moves from high potential to low potential (in the direction of the electric field), losing potential energy and gaining kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where is the electric field strongest near a point charge?

options.text: At an infinite distance from the charge
At all distances it is the same value
Very close to the charge where lines are densest
At exactly one meter away from the charge

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric field strength E = kQ/r² is greatest close to the charge, where field lines are most densely packed. As distance increases, the field weakens.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'electric potential difference' (voltage)?

options.text: The work done per unit charge moving between two points in a field
The total charge stored on a capacitor's conducting plate surface
The rate at which charge flows through a wire in one second
The force experienced by a unit charge at a point in the field

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric potential difference (voltage) is the work done per unit charge when moving a charge between two points: ΔV = W/q.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'electric field line'?

options.text: A physical wire through which electric current flows in a circuit
An imaginary line showing the direction a positive charge would move
The boundary between a positively charged and negatively charged region
A line connecting points that have the same electric potential value

quiz_questions.explanation: An electric field line is an imaginary line that shows the direction of the force on a positive test charge. Lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'equipotential surface'?

options.text: A surface where the electric field has its maximum possible strength
A surface where every point has the same electric potential value
A surface that separates positive charges from negative charges
A surface where no electric charges can exist under any conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: An equipotential surface connects all points at the same electric potential. No work is done moving a charge along an equipotential surface, and these surfaces are always perpendicular to field lines.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'potential energy' mean in the context of electric fields?

options.text: The energy a charged particle has due to its position in an electric field
The kinetic energy a charged particle gains as it accelerates in a field
The thermal energy produced when current flows through a resistance wire
The energy stored in the magnetic field around a current-carrying wire

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric potential energy is the energy stored by a charge due to its position in an electric field. U = qV, where q is the charge and V is the potential at that point.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'uniform electric field'?

options.text: A field that exists only in one small region of space around a charge
A field that has the same strength and direction everywhere in a region
A field that changes direction randomly at every point throughout space
A field produced by a single point charge located at the origin point

quiz_questions.explanation: A uniform electric field has constant magnitude and direction throughout a region. It is represented by parallel, equally spaced field lines. The field between parallel charged plates is approximately uniform.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'test charge' used for in physics?

options.text: A large charge used to create the electric field being measured
A small positive charge used to measure the field without disturbing it
A neutral particle used to detect magnetic fields around current wires
An electron used to create sparks in a static electricity experiment

quiz_questions.explanation: A test charge is an idealized small positive charge used to probe an electric field. It must be small enough not to disturb the field it is measuring. E = F/q gives the field from the force on the test charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'potential' mean in the phrase 'electric potential'?

options.text: The ability to do work on a charge if it were placed at that point
The actual kinetic energy of charges moving through the field region
The magnetic force experienced by a moving charge in the region
The resistance to charge flow measured in ohms at that point

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric potential at a point represents the ability (potential) to do work on a charge placed there. A higher potential means more work can be done per unit charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'field strength' mean for an electric field?

options.text: The total charge creating the field in a given region of space
The force per unit charge experienced at a particular point in space
The voltage difference between the two plates of a parallel capacitor
The number of field lines drawn in a standard physics diagram per area

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric field strength is the force per unit positive charge at a point: E = F/q. It is measured in N/C or V/m and indicates how strong the electric influence is at that location.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'work done' in moving a charge through a potential difference?

options.text: W = qV, the product of charge and potential difference it moves through
W = qE, the product of charge and the electric field strength value
W = q/V, the ratio of charge to the potential difference encountered
W = V/q, the ratio of potential difference to the charge being moved

quiz_questions.explanation: The work done moving charge q through a potential difference ΔV is W = qΔV. This equals the change in electric potential energy of the charge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'superposition' in the context of electric fields?

options.text: Electric fields from multiple charges add up as vectors at any point
Electric fields from multiple charges cancel each other out completely
Only the strongest electric field matters and all others are ignored
Electric fields can only exist one at a time in any region of space

quiz_questions.explanation: The principle of superposition states that the total electric field at a point is the vector sum of the fields from all individual charges. This allows calculating fields from complex charge distributions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a positive test charge near a large positive charge. In which direction does the test charge move?

options.text: Toward the large positive charge along the field line
Away from the large positive charge along the field line
In a circular orbit around the large positive charge
It does not move because both charges are positive

quiz_questions.explanation: Like charges repel. The positive test charge experiences a force away from the large positive charge, moving in the direction of the electric field lines (which point away from positive charges).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student draws field lines around a negative charge. Which way do the lines point?

options.text: The field lines point outward away from the negative charge
The field lines point inward toward the negative charge
The field lines form closed loops around the negative charge
The field lines point upward regardless of the charge position

quiz_questions.explanation: Electric field lines always point toward negative charges and away from positive charges. A positive test charge would be attracted toward the negative charge, so the field lines point inward.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student measures the potential difference between two points as 12 V. If they move a 2 C charge between those points, how much work is done?

options.text: The work done is 6 joules of energy
The work done is 24 joules of energy
The work done is 14 joules of energy
The work done is 10 joules of energy

quiz_questions.explanation: W = qΔV = 2 C × 12 V = 24 J. The work done equals the charge times the potential difference.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes that field lines between two parallel plates are straight and equally spaced. What does this indicate about the field?

options.text: The field is strongest near the edges of the parallel plates
The field is zero everywhere between the two parallel plates
The field is uniform with constant strength and direction between them
The field is random and changes direction at every point between them

quiz_questions.explanation: Parallel, equally spaced field lines indicate a uniform electric field — the field has the same strength and direction at every point between the plates. E = V/d for parallel plates.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student compares the field from a single positive charge to the field from two positive charges side by side. How does the field between the two charges behave?

options.text: The fields add together to create an extra strong field between them
The fields partially cancel between the charges since they point in opposite directions
The field between them is identical to that of a single charge alone
There is no electric field anywhere between two positive charges at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Between two positive charges, the electric fields point in opposite directions (away from each charge). By superposition, they partially cancel, creating a weaker field between them with a null point at the midpoint if charges are equal.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student releases a negative charge from rest in a uniform electric field pointing to the right. Which way does the charge move?

options.text: To the right, in the direction of the electric field
To the left, opposite to the direction of the electric field
Upward, perpendicular to the direction of the electric field
It does not move because the field is uniform throughout

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative charge experiences a force opposite to the field direction. Since the field points right, the force on the negative charge is to the left, and it accelerates leftward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wants to move a positive charge from a low-potential region to a high-potential region. Does this require external work?

options.text: No, the charge naturally moves from low to high potential on its own
Yes, external work is needed because this is against the electric force
No, electric potential has nothing to do with work done on charges
Yes, but only if the charge is moving faster than a certain speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Positive charges naturally move from high to low potential. Moving one from low to high potential is against the electric force direction, requiring external work — just like lifting an object against gravity.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student doubles the distance from a point charge. By what factor does the electric field change?

options.text: The field decreases to half of its original strength
The field decreases to one quarter of its original strength
The field stays exactly the same regardless of distance
The field increases to four times its original strength

quiz_questions.explanation: E = kQ/r². Doubling r gives E = kQ/(2r)² = kQ/4r², which is one quarter of the original field. The electric field follows the inverse square law.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that no work is done when moving a charge along an equipotential surface. Why is this the case?

options.text: Because the charge has zero mass when on an equipotential surface
Because there is no potential difference between points on the surface
Because the electric field is zero everywhere on an equipotential surface
Because equipotential surfaces only exist in regions with no charges at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Work W = qΔV. On an equipotential surface, ΔV = 0, so W = 0. No work is done because there is no change in potential between any two points on the same surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a Van de Graaff generator to charge a metal sphere. They notice sparks jump to their hand when they bring it close. What causes the sparking?

options.text: The metal sphere gets hot enough to produce visible thermal radiation
The strong electric field ionizes the air creating a conducting path for discharge
Magnetic fields from the generator push electrons through the air gap
Light waves from the generator are mistaken for sparks by the observer

quiz_questions.explanation: The high potential on the sphere creates a strong electric field near its surface. When this field is strong enough, it ionizes air molecules, creating a conducting path through which charge rapidly discharges as a visible spark.
